schools.summary: The absolute beginning: 'in the beginning (bereshith) God created (bara) the heaven and the earth' — the absolute commencement of the created order (the beginning of time and of all things, before it only the eternal God); the verb 'created' (bara), used distinctively of God's making, denoting the bringing into being of what did not exist (the idea of creation out of nothing, ex nihilo, contained in the form and use of the word, God producing the universe by his word and will, not from pre-existing matter); 'the heaven and the earth' the comprehensive expression for the whole created universe (the totality of all things) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott.

Calvin and Gill: God, the eternal Creator, brought the heaven and earth into being out of nothing (the noble idea, as Clarke notes, of producing something from nothing, marking the divine, omnipotent act); 'Elohim,' the plural-form name of God joined to the singular verb 'created,' the eternal God the sole Creator of all; the verse the foundation of the doctrine of creation ex nihilo, against the notion of eternal matter.

Basil the Great (Hexaemeron): the verse is the foundation of all — the visible universe created by God in the beginning, out of nothing, the work then unfolded in the six days; 'the heaven and the earth' the totality of the created order, brought into being by the will and word of the eternal God, the Creator distinct from and prior to his creation, against the philosophers who held matter eternal.

themes: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth
the creation of the universe by God in the beginning
creation ex nihilo by the eternal God
the absolute commencement of all things

schools.summary: The initial state: 'and the earth was without form, and void (tohu va-bohu); and darkness was upon the face of the deep (tehom). And the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters' — the earth, newly created, at first an unformed, empty, chaotic mass (not yet shaped, ordered, or filled, the raw material out of which God would form and furnish the world, darkness covering the watery abyss); 'the Spirit of God moved (rachaph — brooded, hovered) upon the face of the waters' the divine creative power brooding over the unformed mass to quicken and order the chaos (some render 'a wind of God,' but the personal Spirit, the divine creative power cherishing and preparing the chaos, is the fuller reading) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott.

Calvin and Gill: the deep (the primeval waters) covered with darkness, the earth lying in a confused, dark, watery state awaiting God's forming word (the chaos not eternal but a stage in God's creative work); 'the Spirit of God' the Holy Spirit, who brooded or hovered over the waters (like a bird over its nest), cherishing and quickening the unformed mass, sustaining and preparing it for the order to come — the Spirit's brooding the secret, vivifying presence preparing the chaos for creation.

Basil the Great: the Spirit of God moving over the waters is the Holy Spirit, brooding over and quickening the unformed mass, cherishing and preparing it as a bird warms its eggs — the Spirit's vivifying, fostering presence imparting life-giving power to the waters, the divine Spirit's work in the forming of the creation, the chaos prepared by the Spirit for the order God will bring forth in the days.

themes: the earth was without form, and void; and darkness was upon the face of the deep
the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters
the unformed earth and the Spirit moving on the waters
the dark chaos quickened by the brooding Spirit

schools.summary: The creative word: 'and God said, Let there be light: and there was light' — God creating by his word, the first creative fiat (Psalm 33:6, 9, 'he spake, and it was done'); light called into being by the divine command (the word of God the efficient cause of creation, the New Testament identifying this Word as the eternal Son, John 1:1-3, through whom all things were made); 'and there was light' the instant, efficacious effect of the divine word (light coming at once at God's command, the immediacy and efficacy of the divine creative speech, the word spoken and the thing done marking the omnipotence of God's creative word) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Henry.

Calvin and Gill: light the first thing created in the ordering of the world (before the sun, made on the fourth day, God bringing forth light, the light however produced dispelling the primeval darkness, the beginning of order and beauty in the creation); God's word the efficient and omnipotent cause, the light obeying his command at once — the creation of light by the bare word of God displaying his power and the dependence of all things on his will.

themes: And God said, Let there be light: and there was light
the creation of light by God's word
the first creative fiat, the word the efficient cause
light called into being before the sun

schools.summary: The divine approval and separation: 'and God saw the light, that it was good: and God divided the light from the darkness' — God surveying his work and pronouncing it good (the refrain 'it was good,' recurring through the chapter, marking the perfection, fitness, and beauty of each work, the creation answering God's design and approved by him, God's seeing good his approbation — not that he learned anything but that he pronounces and establishes its goodness); 'God divided (separated) the light from the darkness' (God ordering the light and darkness into a distinction, assigning each its place and time, day and night, v.5, the first act of separating and ordering the creation, light and darkness distinguished by God's appointment) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: God saw the light, that it was good: and God divided the light from the darkness
God seeing the light good and dividing it from darkness
the divine approval of the creation
the first act of ordering light and darkness

schools.summary: Calvin and Gill (the traditional reading): 'God called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night. And the evening and the morning were the first day' — God naming the light and darkness, assigning them their names and appointed alternation (the naming marking God's ordering and dominion over his works); the 'day' a natural day of twenty-four hours (the evening and morning making one ordinary day, the vicissitude of light and darkness in the space of a day, the six days six literal days, the days of creation real, successive natural days).

Ellicott (the day-age reading): the creative 'day' is 'not a period of [twenty-four] hours, but an æon, or period of indefinite duration,' as the Bible elsewhere uses 'day' indefinitely (the days as long, successive periods of God's creative work, the creation unfolding in great stages); a third view (Augustine, noted by Ellicott) takes the days figuratively, as the order of creation rather than literal time, or an instantaneous creation framed in days for human understanding. Keil and Delitzsch take the days as real creative days marked by evening and morning, the measure of created time.

Basil the Great (Hexaemeron): reflects reverently on 'one day' as the divinely appointed first measure of created time, the day completed by evening and morning; he treats the day as the foundational unit of time established by God at creation, the first day the beginning of the measured succession of days, without resolving it into mere ages — the day a real, God-ordained measure.

debates.question: How long is the creative 'day' (evening and morning, the first day)?

sides.position: A literal natural day of twenty-four hours — the six days six ordinary days
An æon or period of indefinite duration — the day-age view, the days long successive periods
Figurative or instantaneous — the days the order of creation, not literal time (Augustine)

themes: God called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night. And the evening and the morning were the first day
the naming of day and night and the first day
the crux of the length of the creative day
literal twenty-four-hour day vs age vs figurative

schools.summary: The firmament: 'and God said, Let there be a firmament (raqia, an expanse) in the midst of the waters, and let it divide the waters from the waters' — God creating the firmament, the expanse (the sky/atmosphere) spread out in the midst of the primeval waters (the word raqia denoting something stretched or beaten out, the expanse of heaven, God's word bringing forth the expanse that orders the waters); 'let it divide the waters from the waters' (the firmament separating the waters below — seas, rivers, the waters on the earth — from the waters above — the clouds, the atmospheric waters, God ordering the waters by the expanse, the second act of separation in the creation after light and darkness, the waters divided and assigned their places) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin.

Basil the Great (Hexaemeron): the firmament is the expanse of the heavens (the atmosphere or the visible sky), not necessarily a solid vault but the spread-out region that separates the waters; Basil reflects on the nature of this expanse as God's appointed division of the waters, the firmament the second day's work by which God orders the waters above and below, the heavens stretched out at God's command.

themes: Let there be a firmament in the midst of the waters, and let it divide the waters from the waters
the creation of the firmament dividing the waters
the expanse of heaven spread out to order the waters
the second act of separation in the creation

schools.summary: The firmament made: 'and God made the firmament, and divided the waters which were under the firmament from the waters which were above the firmament: and it was so' — God carrying out the word of v.6, making the expanse and dividing the waters into those below (on and around the earth) and those above (the atmospheric waters/clouds), the command of v.6 accomplished in the act of v.7; the waters divided by the expanse (the waters below — seas, the deep — and the waters above — the clouds, suspended in the heavens, the firmament holding the upper and lower waters apart, the ordering of the waters by God's appointment); 'and it was so' the efficacy of the divine word (what God commanded coming to pass, the recurring 'it was so' marking the unfailing efficacy of God's creative word, the work done as God spoke) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: God made the firmament, and divided the waters which were under the firmament from the waters which were above the firmament: and it was so
the making of the firmament and the dividing of the waters
the waters ordered below and above by the expanse
the efficacy of God's creative word

schools.summary: The firmament named Heaven: 'and God called the firmament Heaven. And the evening and the morning were the second day' — God naming the expanse 'Heaven' (shamayim), as he named the light Day (v.5), the naming marking his ordering and dominion (the firmament/expanse the visible heaven, the sky above, the expanse that the eye beholds, God giving it its name and place in the ordered creation); 'the evening and the morning were the second day' the close of the second day (the refrain, as v.5), notably the approval 'it was good' not stated for the second day (the work of dividing the waters completed only with the gathering of the seas on the third day, v.10, the second day closing with the firmament made) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: God called the firmament Heaven. And the evening and the morning were the second day
the firmament named Heaven and the second day
the expanse named the visible heaven
the close of the second day's work

schools.summary: The gathering of the waters: 'and God said, Let the waters under the heaven be gathered together unto one place, and let the dry land appear: and it was so' — God commanding the waters below the firmament to be gathered into one place (the seas), so that the dry land may appear (the ordering and confining of the waters into their bounds, the separation of land and sea); 'let the dry land appear' (the earth, before covered by the waters, emerging, the dry land uncovered and made fit for habitation and vegetation, the separation of land from sea preparing the earth for life); 'and it was so' the efficacy of the command (the waters obeying their bounds at God's word) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Henry, Gill.

Basil the Great (Hexaemeron): the gathering of the waters into one place and the emergence of the dry land is the work of God's word, the waters obeying their appointed bounds and the land appearing at his command; Basil reflects on the marvel of the sea confined to its place and the earth uncovered and made ready, the divine word ordering sea and land, the waters running to their gathering and the dry land brought forth to be the seat of life.

themes: Let the waters under the heaven be gathered together unto one place, and let the dry land appear: and it was so
the gathering of the seas and the appearing of dry land
the waters confined to their bounds and the earth uncovered
the third day's first work, the earth made ready for life

schools.summary: The naming and approval: 'and God called the dry land Earth; and the gathering together of the waters called he Seas: and God saw that it was good' — God naming the dry land 'Earth' and the gathered waters 'Seas,' as he named the Day and Heaven (the naming marking his ordering and dominion over the land and sea, the elements receiving their names and bounds, the naming completing the separation of land and water, each given its name, place, and bounds, the ordered distinction of land and sea by God's appointment); 'God saw that it was good' the restored refrain (the approval, omitted on the second day, v.8, now returning, for the separation of the waters is completed with the gathering of the seas, the work of ordering the earth and sea pronounced good, the creation answering God's design) — Keil and Delitzsch, Clarke, Gill.

themes: God called the dry land Earth; and the gathering together of the waters called he Seas: and God saw that it was good
the naming of earth and seas and God's approval
the land and sea named, bounded, and pronounced good
the ordered distinction of land and sea

schools.summary: The creation of vegetation: 'and God said, Let the earth bring forth grass, the herb yielding seed, and the fruit tree yielding fruit after his kind, whose seed is in itself, upon the earth: and it was so' — God commanding the earth to produce vegetation: grass, seed-bearing herbs, and fruit trees (the earth, now uncovered and ordered, made fruitful at God's word, the first living things, the plants, brought forth); 'after his kind, whose seed is in itself' (each species bearing seed to reproduce after its own kind, the principle of reproduction according to kind established at creation, the plants endowed with seed to perpetuate their species, the order and fixity of the kinds) — Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

Basil the Great (Hexaemeron): the word 'let the earth bring forth' endues the earth with fruitfulness — the earth, at God's command, bringing forth vegetation in marvelous variety and order, the once-barren land teeming with plants each after its kind; Basil reflects on the wisdom and bounty of God displayed in the green world, the divine word giving the earth its enduring power to produce, the plants brought forth in their kinds to clothe and feed the world.

themes: Let the earth bring forth grass, the herb yielding seed, and the fruit tree yielding fruit after his kind, whose seed is in itself
the creation of vegetation, each after its kind
reproduction according to kind established at creation
the earth made fruitful at God's word

schools.summary: The earth brings forth: 'and the earth brought forth grass, and herb yielding seed after his kind, and the tree yielding fruit, whose seed was in itself, after his kind: and God saw that it was good' — the earth, at God's word (v.11), bringing forth the vegetation (the command fulfilled, the grass, herbs, and fruit trees appearing, each after its kind, with seed to reproduce, the word of God accomplished in the fruitful earth); the repeated 'after his kind' (each plant bringing forth after its own kind, with its seed in itself, the species distinct and perpetuating themselves, the order of the kinds established at creation maintained in reproduction); 'God saw that it was good' (the vegetation, the work of the third day, pronounced good, the fruitful earth answering God's design, the second 'good' of the third day, with v.10, marking the completeness and fitness of the earth and its plants) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: the earth brought forth grass, and herb yielding seed after his kind, and the tree yielding fruit, whose seed was in itself, after his kind: and God saw that it was good
the earth bringing forth vegetation as commanded
the fixity and perpetuation of the kinds
the fruitful earth pronounced good

schools.summary: The close of the third day: 'and the evening and the morning were the third day' — the third day of creation completed (the refrain, as vv.5, 8), on this day God gathering the seas, uncovering the dry land, and making it bring forth vegetation (the third day's twofold work — the separation of land and sea, and the clothing of the earth with plants — finished); 'the evening and the morning' marking the bounds of the day (as in the previous days, the day completed by its evening and morning, the third in the succession of the creative days); the third day the close of the first triad of days (light, firmament, land and vegetation, the forming and ordering of the realms — heaven, sea, and land — which the second triad, days four to six, will fill with their inhabitants), the third day completing the preparation of the earth as the seat of life — Gill.

themes: And the evening and the morning were the third day
the close of the third day
the third day completing the earth's forming
the close of the first triad of creative days

schools.summary: The heavenly lights: 'and God said, Let there be lights in the firmament of the heaven to divide the day from the night; and let them be for signs, and for seasons, and for days, and years' — God appointing the heavenly lights (luminaries: sun, moon, stars) in the firmament to divide day from night (the lights set to rule and measure the times, the fourth day filling the heavens with their luminaries); 'for signs, and for seasons, and for days, and years' their offices (marking times: signs — of weather, of God's appointments, of seasons; seasons — the fixed times, festivals, agricultural seasons; days and years — the measures of time, the heavenly bodies God's appointed timekeepers for the earth) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

Basil the Great (Hexaemeron), with Calvin: the light created on the first day is now gathered into and ordered by the luminaries — the sun and moon not the source of light's existence but its appointed vessels and rulers; God made the light before the lights, that we might know light depends on him, not on the sun, the lights ministers of the divine appointment; Basil and Calvin reflect that the heavenly bodies are servants of God's order, set to dispense and rule the light, not gods to be worshiped but creatures appointed to measure time and serve the earth.

themes: Let there be lights in the firmament of the heaven to divide the day from the night; and let them be for signs, and for seasons, and for days, and years
the creation of the heavenly lights for signs and seasons
the luminaries appointed as God's timekeepers
light from God, not the sun, ordered into the lights

schools.summary: The lights give light to the earth: 'and let them be for lights in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth: and it was so' — beyond dividing day and night and marking times (v.14), the lights serving to give light upon the earth (their primary office toward the earth illumination, dispensing light to the world for the benefit of its creatures); 'to give light upon the earth' (the sun, moon, and stars illumining the earth, providing the light by which life and labor proceed, the heavens serving the earth, the lights appointed for the earth's good); 'and it was so' (the command accomplished, the lights set to give light as God appointed, the recurring 'it was so' marking the efficacy of God's word, the luminaries fulfilling their office at his command) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: let them be for lights in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth: and it was so
the lights set to give light upon the earth
the heavens serving the earth with light
the luminaries fulfilling their office at God's word

schools.summary: The two great lights and the stars: 'and God made two great lights; the greater light to rule the day, and the lesser light to rule the night: he made the stars also' — God appointing the two great lights, the sun (the greater light) to govern the day and the moon (the lesser light) the night ('great' not in absolute size but in appearance and office toward the earth, the sun and moon the chief luminaries set to rule the times); 'he made the stars also' (the countless stars made by God, added almost as in passing yet displaying the vastness of his work, the stars set in the heavens to adorn them and to serve) — Clarke, Ellicott.

Calvin: the sun and moon called the 'two great lights' as they appear to us and serve us — Moses speaking according to common perception, not astronomical exactness; the moon, though smaller than many stars, 'great' in its nearness and office to the earth; God describing his works as they serve human life. With Basil, against the worship of the heavenly bodies: the sun, moon, and stars are God's creatures, not deities, servants of his order set to govern the times and adorn the heavens.

themes: God made two great lights; the greater light to rule the day, and the lesser light to rule the night: he made the stars also
the making of the sun, moon, and stars
the luminaries God's creatures, not deities
the sun and moon great in appearance and office toward the earth

schools.summary: The lights set in the heavens: 'and God set them in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth' — God placing the sun, moon, and stars in the expanse of the heaven, appointing them their stations, that they may give light upon the earth (the lights fixed in their places by God's hand and ordained to serve the earth with light); 'set them in the firmament of the heaven' (God giving them their places and courses in the heavens, the lights not wandering by chance but established by God in their appointed stations, the order of the heavens God's arrangement); 'to give light upon the earth' (the repeated purpose, vv.15-17, emphasizing that the heavens serve the earth, the lights appointed for the earth's illumination, the heavenly bodies ministering to the world below) — Gill.

themes: God set them in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth
God setting the lights in the heavens to illumine the earth
the lights established in their appointed stations
the heavens serving the earth with light

schools.summary: The lights rule and are approved: 'and to rule over the day and over the night, and to divide the light from the darkness: and God saw that it was good' — the lights set to govern the times (the sun governing the day and the moon and stars the night, the lights ruling and ordering the times, dividing light from darkness, the heavenly bodies God's appointed governors of day and night); 'to rule over the day and over the night' (the sun and moon 'ruling' day and night, not as conscious rulers but as appointed governors of the times, presiding over and measuring the day and night by God's ordinance, the order of the times in their keeping); 'God saw that it was good' (the work of the fourth day, the lights, pronounced good, the heavens furnished with their luminaries answering God's design, the refrain of approval marking the fitness and beauty of the heavenly order) — Gill.

themes: to rule over the day and over the night, and to divide the light from the darkness: and God saw that it was good
the lights ruling day and night and the divine approval
the heavenly bodies appointed governors of the times
the heavens pronounced good

schools.summary: The close of the fourth day: 'and the evening and the morning were the fourth day' — the fourth day of creation completed (the refrain, as vv.5, 8, 13), on this day God furnishing the heavens with the sun, moon, and stars, appointing them to divide and rule the times (the fourth day's work finished); 'the evening and the morning' marking the bounds of the day (the day completed by its evening and morning, the fourth in the succession of the creative days); the fourth day opening the second triad of days (lights, fish and fowl, beasts and man — the filling of the realms formed in the first triad), as the first day made light and the fourth made the lights, the days corresponding, the fourth day beginning the furnishing of the heavens and the times with their rulers — Gill.

themes: And the evening and the morning were the fourth day
the close of the fourth day
the heavens furnished with the sun, moon, and stars
the fourth day opening the second triad of filling days

schools.summary: The creation of sea-life and birds: 'and God said, Let the waters bring forth abundantly the moving creature that hath life, and fowl that may fly above the earth in the open firmament of heaven' — God commanding the waters to teem with living creatures (the first animal life, the swarming creatures of the sea, the waters bringing forth abundantly at God's word, the seas filled with their inhabitants); 'the moving creature that hath life (a living soul, nephesh)' the sea-creatures endowed with animal life, a living principle beyond the plants (the first creatures bearing the 'living soul,' the abundance and variety of sea-life brought forth); 'fowl that may fly above the earth in the open firmament of heaven' (the birds made to fly in the open expanse) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

Basil the Great (Hexaemeron): reflects on the marvelous variety of the winged creatures and the fish, the bounty and wisdom of God filling the waters and the air with life; the fifth day filling the sea and sky with their living inhabitants, the swarming abundance of the waters and the manifold kinds of birds displaying the creative power and goodness of God, who endues the waters with the power to bring forth living souls and fills the open firmament with flying creatures.

themes: Let the waters bring forth abundantly the moving creature that hath life, and fowl that may fly above the earth in the open firmament of heaven
the creation of fish and fowl from the waters
the seas and sky filled with living inhabitants
the first animal life endowed with the living soul

schools.summary: The creation of sea-creatures and birds: 'and God created great whales, and every living creature that moveth, which the waters brought forth abundantly, after their kind, and every winged fowl after his kind: and God saw that it was good' — the word 'created' (bara, as in v.1) used again, marking the bringing forth of animal life as a fresh, distinctive creative act (the great sea-creatures and all the swarming life of the waters God's creation); 'great whales (tanninim)' the great sea-monsters or sea-creatures (the largest of the creatures specially mentioned, displaying the power of God, the great and small alike his work); 'after their kind... and God saw that it was good' (the creatures made after their kind, each species distinct and reproducing after its kind, the fifth day's work pronounced good, the living world answering God's design) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: God created great whales, and every living creature that moveth, which the waters brought forth abundantly, after their kind, and every winged fowl after his kind: and God saw that it was good
the creation of the great sea-creatures and birds after their kind
the word bara marking the creation of animal life
the living world of sea and air pronounced good

schools.summary: The blessing of the creatures: 'and God blessed them, saying, Be fruitful, and multiply, and fill the waters in the seas, and let fowl multiply in the earth' — God blessing the sea-creatures and birds, endowing them with the power and command to be fruitful, multiply, and fill the seas and the earth (the first blessing in Scripture, the gift of fruitfulness and increase to the living creatures); 'be fruitful, and multiply' both a blessing and an ordinance (God granting and commanding the creatures to propagate their kinds, the power of reproduction and increase a divine gift, the creatures filling their realms by God's blessing); 'fill the waters in the seas, and let fowl multiply in the earth' (the sea and sky to be filled with their inhabitants, God's blessing ensuring the increase and abundance of life, the realms formed in the first triad filled in the second) — Clarke, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: God blessed them, saying, Be fruitful, and multiply, and fill the waters in the seas, and let fowl multiply in the earth
God blessing the creatures to multiply
the first blessing of Scripture, the gift of fruitfulness
the filling of sea and sky with life

schools.summary: The close of the fifth day: 'and the evening and the morning were the fifth day' — the fifth day of creation completed (the refrain, as the previous days), on this day God filling the waters with sea-creatures and the air with birds, and blessing them to multiply (the fifth day's work finished); 'the evening and the morning' marking the bounds of the day (the day completed by its evening and morning, the fifth in the succession of the creative days); the fifth day, the second of the filling triad, corresponding to the second day (the firmament dividing the waters) — as the second day made the sea and sky, the fifth fills them with fish and fowl, the second triad filling the realms of the first, the fifth day furnishing the waters and the air with their living inhabitants — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And the evening and the morning were the fifth day
the close of the fifth day
the sea and air filled with life
the fifth day corresponding to the second

schools.summary: The creation of land animals: 'and God said, Let the earth bring forth the living creature after his kind, cattle, and creeping thing, and beast of the earth after his kind: and it was so' — God commanding the earth to bring forth the land animals (the living creatures of the dry land, after their kinds, the sixth day beginning with the furnishing of the earth with its animal inhabitants); the threefold classes: 'cattle (the domesticable animals), and creeping thing (the smaller creatures, reptiles, insects), and beast of the earth (the wild beasts)' — the land animals in their kinds, tame and wild, great and small, the earth furnished with its varied animal life; 'after his kind... and it was so' (each species distinct and reproducing after its kind, the command accomplished, the earth bringing forth its creatures at God's word) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

Basil the Great (Hexaemeron): reflects on the variety, instincts, and order of the land animals as the work of God's wisdom — the cattle, creeping things, and wild beasts each endowed with their natures and instincts by the Creator, the earth bringing forth its living creatures at God's command; the manifold kinds of animals displaying the wisdom and providence of God, who fits each creature to its place and gives the earth the power to bring forth living souls after their kinds.

themes: Let the earth bring forth the living creature after his kind, cattle, and creeping thing, and beast of the earth after his kind: and it was so
the creation of the land animals after their kind
the threefold classes of land animals, tame and wild
the earth furnished with its varied animal life

schools.summary: The land animals made: 'and God made the beast of the earth after his kind, and cattle after their kind, and every thing that creepeth upon the earth after his kind: and God saw that it was good' — God carrying out the word of v.24, making the land animals in their three classes (wild beasts, cattle, creeping things), each after its kind (the command accomplished in the act, the earth furnished with its animal inhabitants); the repeated 'after his kind' (each animal made and reproducing after its own kind, the species distinct and perpetuating themselves, the order of the kinds established at creation maintained among the animals); 'God saw that it was good' (the land animals pronounced good, the animal creation answering God's design and approved by him, the work good before man is made) — Keil and Delitzsch, Clarke, Gill.

Basil the Great (Hexaemeron): reflects on the goodness and fitness of each creature in its kind — the land animals made by God and pronounced good, each fitted to its nature and place; the animal creation displaying the wisdom and goodness of the Creator, the beasts, cattle, and creeping things good in themselves and in their order, the whole made ready before God makes man to rule over them.

themes: God made the beast of the earth after his kind, and cattle after their kind, and every thing that creepeth upon the earth after his kind: and God saw that it was good
the making of the land animals and the divine approval
the fixity and perpetuation of the kinds
the animal creation good before the making of man

schools.summary: Gill, Calvin, and Henry (the Christian/Trinitarian reading): 'Let us make man in our image, after our likeness' the language of the plurality of persons in the Godhead — God the Father speaking to the Son and the Holy Spirit, the consultation within the Trinity over the making of man (Henry: 'the three persons of the Godhead'), the plural marking the divine counsel of the Triune God; man made 'in the image of God' consisting chiefly in the rational, immortal soul and in the moral likeness of knowledge, righteousness, and true holiness (Ephesians 4:24; Colossians 3:10, the soul especially bearing God's image, man made godlike in nature and originally in holiness); the dominion over the creatures part of the image (man as God's vicegerent over the earth, the rule belonging to man as bearing God's image, 'them,' male and female, v.27, showing the dominion given to mankind).

Clarke (with Wesley): 'Let us make man' shows a marked deliberation and consultation, the language of the persons of the Godhead over the making of man, distinct from the simple fiats of the earlier works; man made in the image of God in his rational, immortal soul and moral likeness, and given dominion over the creatures, the crown and lord of the earthly creation, made by the special counsel of God.

Gill notes and rejects two alternative readings of the plural: the Jewish 'plural of majesty/honour' (Saadiah, God speaking as kings do, using the plural of dignity), and the view (the Targum of Jonathan) that God addresses the angels — which Gill rejects, since man was not made in the angels' image nor by their power, the work of creation being God's alone. Ellicott notes the dominion granted to man, made in God's image, as ruler over the creatures, the destined lord of the earth.

debates.question: Whom does God address in 'Let us make man in our image'?

sides.position: The plurality of persons in the Godhead — the Father to the Son and the Holy Spirit, the counsel of the Triune God (the Christian reading)
The plural of majesty or honour — God speaking as kings do (the Jewish reading)
God addressing the angels / the heavenly court
holders: the Targum of Jonathan (noted and rejected by Gill)

themes: Let us make man in our image, after our likeness
let them have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air
the making of man in God's image (the crux of 'let us make man')
man made in God's image to have dominion over the creatures

schools.summary: The creation of man in God's image: 'so God created man in his own image, in the image of God created he him; male and female created he them' — God carrying out the resolve of v.26, creating man in his own image (the threefold repetition of 'created,' bara, and 'image' emphasizing the dignity and distinctiveness of man's creation, man the crown of the creation, made godlike, the only creature bearing the divine image); 'in the image of God' the special dignity of man (the rational soul, the moral likeness of knowledge, righteousness, and holiness, and the dominion, the emphatic repetition stressing that man and man alone bears God's image, the foundation of human dignity and worth); 'male and female created he them' (God creating mankind as male and female, both sexes bearing the image of God, the distinction of male and female God's creation, the basis of marriage and the propagation of the race, v.28) — Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: So God created man in his own image, in the image of God created he him; male and female created he them
the creation of man, male and female, in God's image
man the only creature bearing the divine image
both sexes God's handiwork, the dignity of man established

schools.summary: The blessing and commission of man: 'and God blessed them, and God said unto them, Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth, and subdue it: and have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over every living thing that moveth upon the earth' — God blessing man (as he blessed the creatures, v.22), endowing him with the power and command to multiply and fill (replenish) the earth (the gift of fruitfulness and the ordinance of marriage and family, mankind to people the earth); 'subdue it' the mandate to bring the earth under cultivation and use (to till, develop, and master the earth for human good, man set over the earth to govern and use it, the cultural mandate); 'have dominion over the fish... fowl... and every living thing' the rule over all the creatures, the prerogative of the image of God (v.26, man constituted God's vicegerent over the earth and its creatures, the dominion a trust under God, the creatures given for man's service and care) — Clarke, Calvin, Gill.

themes: God blessed them... Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth, and subdue it
have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over every living thing
the blessing of man with fruitfulness and dominion
man set as God's vicegerent over the earth

schools.summary: The gift of food: 'and God said, Behold, I have given you every herb bearing seed, which is upon the face of all the earth, and every tree, in the which is the fruit of a tree yielding seed; to you it shall be for meat' — God granting man the seed-bearing herbs and the fruit trees for food (the original provision for mankind the plants — herbs and fruits, God's bounty supplying man's need from the produce of the earth); 'to you it shall be for meat (food)' (the herbs and fruits appointed as man's food, in the original creation the diet given to man vegetable, the eating of flesh granted later, after the flood, 9:3, God providing for man's sustenance from the fruitful earth); 'Behold, I have given you' (God, having made man lord of the earth, providing for his maintenance, the gift the token of God's fatherly care, man to live by God's bounty, dependent on the Creator who supplies his food) — Clarke, Ellicott, Henry, Gill.

themes: Behold, I have given you every herb bearing seed... and every tree, in the which is the fruit of a tree yielding seed; to you it shall be for meat
God's gift of the plants for food
the original vegetable diet, God's provision for man
the bounty of the earth supplying man's sustenance

schools.summary: The gift of food to the animals: 'and to every beast of the earth, and to every fowl of the air, and to every thing that creepeth upon the earth, wherein there is life, I have given every green herb for meat: and it was so' — God providing for the animals as for man (the green herbs, the grasses and plants, given to the beasts, birds, and creeping things for their food, God's bounty supplying the food of all the living creatures); 'wherein there is life (a living soul)' (the provision for all the creatures that have animal life, God caring for the maintenance of all his living creatures, providing their food from the earth); 'I have given every green herb for meat: and it was so' (in the original creation, the food of the animals, as of man, the produce of the earth, God's providence supplying the food of all, the provision established as God appointed, God feeding all his creatures) — Gill.

themes: to every beast of the earth, and to every fowl of the air, and to every thing that creepeth upon the earth, wherein there is life, I have given every green herb for meat
God's gift of the green herbs to the animals for food
God's providence supplying the food of all his creatures
the Creator's care for all the living

schools.summary: The final survey and close: 'and God saw every thing that he had made, and, behold, it was very good. And the evening and the morning were the sixth day' — God surveying the whole completed creation and pronouncing it 'very good' (the individual works each 'good,' vv.4, 10, etc., but the whole, complete and harmonious, with man at its crown, 'very good,' the perfection and goodness of the finished creation, the addition of 'very' marking the completeness and harmony of the whole, the creation as it came from God's hand wholly good, without flaw or evil); 'the evening and the morning were the sixth day' (the sixth day, the climax of the creative week, completed, on it God making the land animals and man, the crown of creation, the work of creation finished, the seventh day, the rest, following, the sixth day completing the six days of God's creative work) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Henry.

themes: God saw every thing that he had made, and, behold, it was very good. And the evening and the morning were the sixth day
God's survey of all his work as very good and the close of the sixth day
the whole creation complete and crowned with man, pronounced very good
the six days of God's creative work finished
